channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Abandoned by Allies, Platner Faces Pressure to End Senate Campaign
item.description: After Graham Platner, the Democratic nominee in Maine, was accused of rape, much of the party and several key supporters turned against him.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Calls mounted for Graham Platner to end his Senate campaign.

item.title: In CNN Interview, Woman Details Alleged Sexual Assault by Graham Platner
item.description: “This is something that I tried for many years to forget,” the woman, Jenny Racicot, said on CNN.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner has faced calls from Democrats to drop out of the Maine Senate race after an allegation of sexual assault by Jenny Racicot, a woman he once dated.

item.title: Who Might Replace Graham Platner if He Drops Out of the Maine Senate Race?
item.description: Graham Platner can be replaced on the ballot if he withdraws in the next week. If he does, Maine Democrats would face an uncertain two-week race to choose a replacement.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner won the Democratic nomination for Senate in early June, but he could still be replaced on the ballot.

item.title: What to Watch at the NATO Summit, and Platner’s Allies Rescind Endorsements
item.description: Plus, the museum that wants you to touch the art.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Ben Hubbard and Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Umit Bektas/Reuters
item.media:description: Leaders of NATO countries are meeting in Ankara, the capital of Turkey, for an annual summit starting on Tuesday.

item.title: In NATO’s Next Act, Can Europe Play the Leading Role?
item.description: The focus at a leaders’ summit in Turkey this week will be on building a new model for the alliance, as President Trump pushes to do less and pressures others do more.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger and Lara Jakes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: NATO leaders at last year’s summit in The Hague, where member states committed to increase spending on defense sharply by 2035.

item.title: Trump Expected to Tell Turkey He Is Ready to Restore Access to F-35 Jets
item.description: The president, who is headed to a NATO summit in Ankara this week, had imposed the ban himself amid concerns that giving Turkey the jets could allow Russia to learn about their stealth technology.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and David E. Sanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: A model of an F-35 in the Oval Office. Turkey has been lobbying furiously for readmission to the fighter jet program for years.

item.title: Inside Trump’s Ideological Fight With the Smithsonian
item.description: Publicly and behind the scenes, the president continues to try to impose his own views of American history and culture, presenting an ongoing challenge to Lonnie Bunch, the institution’s leader.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.category: National Museum of African American History and Culture
item.media:credit: Lawren Simmons for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and his allies have criticized what they call “improper ideology” in the Smithsonian’s presentation of American history and culture.

item.title: Anthea Hartig, Smithsonian Museum Director, Faces the White House’s Wrath
item.description: Historians defended Anthea Hartig, director of the National Museum of American History, after the Trump administration accused her of spreading “radical activist ideology.”
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert/Associated Press
item.media:description: Anthea M. Hartig has been the director of the National Museum of American History since 2019.

item.title: The History at the Smithsonian That the White House Finds Unpatriotic
item.description: The administration’s report criticizing the National Museum of American History echoes themes of President Trump’s push to reshape the American story.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:credit: Lawren Simmons for The New York Times
item.media:description: A model of the Statue of Liberty made by immigrant farmworkers on display at the National Museum of American History in Washington.

item.title: New York’s Best Burgers
item.description: The best of New York’s dynamic dining scene (mostly) between two buns.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times Food Desk, Cole Saladino and Heather Willensky for The New York Times
item.media:credit: Cole Saladino for The New York Times

item.title: McConnell Has Been Hospitalized for 3 Weeks, and Aides Won’t Say Why
item.description: Senator Mitch McConnell was admitted to the hospital on June 14, and his office has provided few updates about his condition. Here’s what we know.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Mitch McConnell, 84, had in recent months used a wheelchair to travel from his office building to the Capitol, although days before his hospitalization he was able to walk on his own.

item.title: How a Four-Generation Cuban Family Survives on $60 a Month
item.description: The U.S. oil blockade has deepened a humanitarian crisis, forcing Cubans to lean on the island’s long tradition of community solidarity to provide a cushion.
item.dc:creator: Ed Augustin and Lisette Poole González
item.media:description: Analeidis Arias Marin gets her children ready for school in Santiago de Cuba in May.

item.title: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Iran’s Slain Leader, Is Mourned in Qom
item.description: The ceremony for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, held at a site considered sacred by many Shiites, was the latest step in a weeklong funeral organized by Iran’s government.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ahmadreza Taheri/WANA News Agency, via Reuters
item.media:description: A state media handout photo is said to show mourners attending a prayer for Iran’s former supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, at the Jamkaran Mosque, in Qom, Iran, on Tuesday.

item.title: Joining a Human Tide of Mourners Flowing Through Tehran’s Streets
item.description: After days of public mourning ceremonies in the Iranian capital, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s coffin arrived in Tehran’s Azadi Square. This is what I saw covering the miles-long procession on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Emile Ducke
item.media:description: Crowds gathered around the coffin of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in Tehran, Iran, on Monday.

item.title: Iranian Strikes on Ships Test Cease-Fire and Threaten Energy Flows in Gulf
item.description: A U.S. official said Iranian missiles struck two commercial ships. There was no immediate comment from Iranian officials on the reported attacks.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt, Qasim Nauman and Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images
item.media:description: Vessels anchored near Muscat, Oman, last month.

item.title: A Long-Awaited Verdict for Marine Le Pen Could Reshape France’s Politics
item.description: A court will decide on Tuesday whether to uphold Ms. Le Pen’s embezzlement conviction. If it does, the far-right leader will probably be barred from running for the presidency next year.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Tom Nicholson/Reuters
item.media:description: Marine Le Pen met with supporters at a country fair in Liévin, France, on Saturday.

item.title: Estonia Won the War on Fentanyl. What Came Next Was Even Worse.
item.description: By 2018, fentanyl overdoses in Estonia had plummeted. But powerful new drugs are appearing fast, with the authorities racing to respond.
item.dc:creator: Azam Ahmed and Meridith Kohut
item.media:description: Long before fentanyl swept American streets, Estonia battled the drug — and won.

item.title: Inside the United States’ Billion-Dollar Blind Spot: Hail
item.description: Scientists have never quite understood how hail works. Meet the storm chasers trying to change that.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times

item.title: Death Toll From Oppressive Heat in New Jersey May Be as High as 29
item.description: In New York City, emergency rooms last week logged the highest number of patients treated for heat-related illnesses in years.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully and Joseph Goldstein
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: Soccer fans sought a cool spot last week at a World Cup match at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey.

item.title: Why China Fired a Long-Range Missile Into the Pacific
item.description: Beijing had long tested nuclear missiles only within its borders. A rare launch over an ocean shows it is determined to narrow the gap with the United States.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas

item.title: The Chinese Company That Could Start a Trade War With Europe
item.description: Nuctech’s rise from a Chinese state-backed start-up to a global border-security supplier has made it a lightning rod in trade and subsidy disputes.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao

item.title: The New York City Plumber Who Made Over $465,000 in a Year
item.description: A public housing employee made more than $465,000 in a year with overtime, while apparently running two private plumbing businesses at the same time. The city is investigating.
item.dc:creator: Mihir Zaveri
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: Many of New York City’s aging public housing developments require constant maintenance. But one plumber working for the public housing agency logged an extraordinary amount of overtime.

item.title: Lessons From the Graham Platner Disaster
item.description: Many Democrats want representatives as furious as they are. That makes it easier for unfit candidates to escape proper vetting.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: A Radical Vision for Israelis and Palestinians
item.description: The old solutions to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict don’t seem to fit the present reality. A Land for All proposes an alternative.
item.dc:creator: Ezra Klein and Jack McCordick
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity

item.title: Caribbean Disparity
item.description: We look at U.S. relief efforts in Haiti in 2010 and in Venezuela now.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Kimmel’ Guest Host Tiffany Haddish Roasts Trump’s Fourth of July Party
item.description: The comedian and actress joked that “there was more people at my bat mitzvah” than at America’s 250th birthday party.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: More Americans Than Ever Are Taking Weight-Loss Drugs
item.description: The number of U.S. adults taking an obesity medication has nearly quadrupled from just two years ago.
item.dc:creator: Dani Blum
item.media:credit: Cydni Elledge for The New York Times
